evidence: All network-facing code in github.ts uses a consistent return-null pattern: fetchJson returns T | null, fetchRepoData returns ProjectGitHubData | null, fetchAllRepoData returns Record<string, ... | null>

Error context is universally discarded: all three catch blocks in github.ts silently return null or swallow with only a comment, losing HTTP status codes, network error details, and rate-limit information
+page.server.ts wraps fetchAllRepoData in a redundant try-catch; the underlying functions already catch and return null, so the outer catch can never fire under normal operation

The commit-count catch block in fetchRepoData (line 52) swallows errors with only a comment ('graceful degradation'), meaning a persistent API issue would silently produce commitCount: 0 with no diagnostic trace
Data files (terminal.ts, artefacts.ts, projects.ts) are pure constant exports with no I/O boundaries, so absence of error handling there is correct

strengths: Consistent return-null strategy across all network fetch functions in github.ts: fetchJson, fetchRepoData, and fetchAllRepoData all follow the same pattern
The architecture explicitly documents that GitHub data is supplementary and static content works without it, making graceful degradation a deliberate design choice rather than accidental
Type signatures honestly reflect the null-return strategy: Promise<T | null> appears consistently, so callers know to expect null
error_consistency.issue_character: The issues are localized to a single module (github.ts and its caller +page.server.ts) and stem from a single root cause: the graceful-degradation strategy discards all error context, making debugging network issues during development or build invisible.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a consistent error strategy (return null for network failures), which is the most important attribute for error_consistency. However, the universal context loss across all catch blocks means a developer debugging build-time GitHub API failures would have no diagnostic information. The redundant try-catch in +page.server.ts suggests the error boundary design is not fully thought through. For a 273-LOC codebase with only one I/O boundary, the issues are modest but real. Score lands around 78: the strategy is coherent but the implementation loses context that would help developers, placing it slightly below the 80 anchor of 'solid but uneven.'

issues.summary: All catch blocks in github.ts discard error context, making network failures invisible during builds
evidence: fetchJson (line 12): catch block returns null with no logging or error wrapping, discarding HTTP status, rate-limit headers, and exception details
fetchRepoData commit-count block (line 52): catch swallows with only a comment, silently producing commitCount: 0 for any failure
Three separate catch blocks, all following the same silent-swallow pattern
issues.suggestion: Add a minimal console.warn in each catch block that includes the URL and error message (e.g., console.warn(`GitHub fetch failed for ${url}:`, error)). This preserves the graceful-degradation behavior while giving developers build-time visibility into API failures, rate limiting, or network issues.

issues.summary: Outer try-catch in +page.server.ts is redundant since fetchAllRepoData already handles all errors internally
evidence: +page.server.ts line 15-19: wraps fetchAllRepoData in try-catch, but fetchAllRepoData delegates to fetchRepoData which delegates to fetchJson, and all failures are already caught and converted to null at the inner layers
The outer catch can only fire if Promise.all itself throws (an engine-level failure) or if the .map/.filter on project arrays throws, neither of which is a network error
issues.suggestion: Either remove the outer try-catch (since the callee already guarantees no throws for network failures) or, if defensive coding at the SvelteKit load boundary is intentional, add a comment explaining that this guards against unexpected non-network failures rather than duplicating the network error handling.

root_causes: The graceful-degradation design philosophy ('GitHub data is supplementary') was implemented as universal silent swallowing rather than as structured fallback with diagnostic output
possible_false_positives: redundant_try_catch_page_server: the outer catch could be an intentional SvelteKit load-boundary guard rather than a redundant network error handler
